data.title: 2 ETF Smart Leverage Portfolio Could Beat The S&P 500 By 200% Over 25 Years
data.description:
What if I told you that you can beat the S&P 500 (SPY) by 200% over the next 25 years? That would mean roughly tripling your ending portfolio balance in 2050 compared to if it were passively invested in the US large-cap equity index — e.g., end up with $1.5 million instead of $500,000. And what if I told you that this can be done rather simply via an investment in only two ETFs at most, and with less risk, as measured by worst declines from all-time highs and worst daily returns?

Ok, to be candid, I cannot promise that we can all achieve results like this. But if any of us wants to give it a try, I have an idea for maximizing the chances of success.

No, I don't have strong opinions or insights about how Bitcoin (GBTC)(BITO), gold (GLD) and silver (SLV), or the semiconductor space (SOXX) will fare in the next few years or decades. I also don't know which asset class or security will see its price skyrocket next. Instead, producing generational wealth well above market pace is likely best approached with at least some multi-asset diversification and, probably more importantly, systematically (and not instinctively) knowing when to use leverage and when to de-risk the portfolio.

Doing the math

Before diving into the details, let's do some quick math to figure out how much annual outperformance vs. the equities benchmark one would need to produce in order to beat the S&P 500 by a cumulative 200% over 25 years. The answer is probably a bit less than many of us might imagine — a byproduct of the power of compounding over the long haul.

Since 1980, dividends included, the S&P 500 has gained 11.5% per year, which is quite impressive. The rise in stock prices has been fueled by a bullish cocktail of falling interest rates, rising valuations, globalization, and the tech productivity boom, among a few other factors. If such gains were replicated over the next two and a half decades (more on this at the end of this article), an initial investment of $100,000 today would grow about 15-fold to $1.5 million by the end of 2050. To end up with a final balance that is 200% higher, i.e., $4.5 million, the necessary annual returns would need to be in the order of 16.5% per year.

While I wouldn't argue that beating the S&P 500 consistently by five percentage points per year is easy, it "feels" doable, assuming a sensible investment strategy that blends aggressiveness and downside risk mitigation.

Busting a myth: leverage increases risk

I want to start our conversation by being a contrarian and challenging the perception that leverage necessarily increases portfolio risk. In some cases, as discussed next, leverage can disproportionally boost upside potential relative to the additional volatility and losses that it may produce, if any.

The secret here is to know when to deploy leverage and when to dial down risk exposure in a systematic way. And here, again, I will be a contrarian: in my view, investors should generally increase leverage when asset prices are near peak levels; and delever (possibly exiting a long position altogether) when asset prices are retreating. You read that right: buy high, sell low — a strategy whose appeal is supported by careful analysis of the historical data, as presented below.

Since 1980, the S&P 500 has produced an annualized volatility of 17.9%. However, this number merely represents an average of good times and bad times in the equities market and deserves to be further dissected.

Volatility tends to be substantially higher when stocks enter correction territory, defined for our purposes today as a 10%-plus pullback from the highs of the prior six months. Also worth noting, volatility tends to increase meaningfully when asset prices go down and decrease when prices go up.

What if I were to split the performance of the S&P 500 between (1) when the index is trading within 10% of its six-month highs (a.k.a., bull periods), and (2) when it dips below such levels (a.k.a., bear periods)? In this case, historical volatility since 1980 would be as follows:

  • During bull periods (81% of the time): 13.8% annualized volatility.
  • During bear periods (19% of the time): 29.5% annualized volatility.

Leverage, which can boost the performance of a portfolio substantially, is most dangerous when deployed during times of high volatility because it amplifies the losses that are inherent in high-volatility periods and it produces the most volatility drag, hence even more losses, which readers can learn more about from sources like the SEC. So, when I look at the volatility numbers above, broken down across bull and bear periods, the first logical thought that crosses my mind is that:

I should feel much more comfortable leveraging my exposure to the S&P 500 when the index is trading within 10% of the highs (which has happened roughly in four out of five trading days, on average), and not the opposite.

Of course, risk is not necessarily the same as volatility. An arguably better definition of risk is the probability of a sizable loss from peak levels that is generally difficult to recover. In this regard, the idea of leveraging the S&P 500 only near highs makes even more sense.

The chart below shows how being divested when the S&P 500 was undergoing a correction would have prevented an investor from partaking in painful bear-market retreats. Those include the 1980s Volcker shock period, the early 2000s dot-com crash, the 2008 Great Financial Crisis, and even Black Monday in 1987 (i.e., all the periods not highlighted in light blue below).

So far, the key takeaway is that, as evidenced by nearly 50 years of historical performance, owning the S&P 500 on leverage makes quite a bit more sense when the index is trading near highs. Generational wealth, therefore, is likely easier to produce when an investor aggressively rides the "beta tailwinds" of market tranquility.

A simple trading strategy

Next, I will propose a minimally diversified portfolio allocation strategy that takes advantage of what I discussed above, properly backtested over the last few decades. The trading idea would follow this straightforward set of rules:

  • During bull periods (i.e., when the S&P 500 is not in a correction), which I expect to be most of the time, split the portfolio 50/50 between longer-duration treasuries and a 3x leveraged S&P 500 position. The former can be achieved today with a simple investment in the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), while the latter would require holding a fund like the ProShares UltraPro S&P500 ETF (UPRO) or the Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X ETF (SPXL). Effectively, this would be a 2x leveraged position between US stocks and government bonds, with the notional being allocated 150% to equities and 50% to treasuries.
  • During bear periods (i.e., when the S&P 500 is in a correction), exit the leveraged S&P 500 position and allocate 100% to TLT.

Below is a chart (in log scale) alongside a brief table that shows how this simple strategy would have fared since 1986 (as far back as I could go to retrieve daily asset prices for both stocks and bonds). Notice that the leveraged portfolio strategy would have outperformed the S&P 500 across the board, other than on annualized volatility: much better absolute returns, better volatility-adjusted returns, tamer maximum drawdown, and much better worst day ever.

It is worth highlighting the 17.1% annual return above. The 6.1 percentage-point advantage over SPY would have been more than enough to beat the equities index by 200% over 25 years, as the title of this article proposes.

Of course, implementing such a trading strategy would require being comfortable holding a leveraged S&P 500 position most of the time, and daily monitoring of the portfolio over many, many years. This is why I think that producing significant alpha, very loosely defined here as absolute returns above the S&P 500, is likely more a function of diligent execution than the sophistication of the investment strategy.

On the other hand, what the smart leverage approach would likely not require is ownership of any more than two highly liquid ETFs during bull periods in equities and a single ETF during bear periods, which underscores the simplicity of the methodology, and without too many trades. Regarding the latter, my backtest suggests that the portfolio allocation would only need to shift between the two ETFs 2.2% of the trading days, or about once every two months, on average.

Looking forward

All the above speak to the strategy applied to the performance of stocks and bonds since the 1980s, which is backward-looking. There is no guarantee, for example, that US stocks will continue to return 11.5% per year indefinitely.

However, I can roughly estimate what the proposed smart leverage strategy might produce in gains between now and 2050. I can start with the treasury piece of the portfolio, which is easier to assess.

Historically, bonds and bond funds have produced gains over their average maturity periods that roughly equal the yield on that particular fixed income instrument or basket of instruments when it was purchased. That is: buying TLT today is likely to result in annual gains of nearly 5.0% through the next 20-plus years, which is the average yield to maturity of the fund.

A quick back-of-the-envelope calculation suggests that, if I allocate 50% of my portfolio to TLT 81% of the time (bull periods) and 100% to the ETF 19% of the time (bear periods), my annual expected return should average about 3.0% in the long run.

For the equity piece, I will anticlimactically use Capital Asset Pricing Model as my north star — not a perfect approach, but one that I will call "good enough" for now. If the 10-year Treasury yield is currently 4.25% and Kroll has set its estimate of the equity risk premium at 5.0%, I might expect SPY to produce 9.25% in gains per year going forward, which I consider reasonable and intuitive.

Next, the data suggests that leveraging the S&P 500 by 1.5x during bull periods and selling the position during bear periods (no allocation to bonds, in this case) has historically increased the annualized return of SPY by an average of 1.2x while maintaining annual volatility roughly the same and cutting maximum drawdown by about three-fourths (see chart below, log scale).

Therefore, I can multiply 1.2 times 9.25% for the equity piece, then add the 3.0% expected return in bonds to estimate that the smart leverage strategy that I proposed earlier might realistically produce 14.0% in annualized returns over a 25-year period, or nearly five percentage points of additional return relative to my expectations of SPY.

At this pace, investors might be able to beat the S&P 500 by roughly 200% cumulatively by 2050 — and improve their chances of retiring with a significantly larger nest egg.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Poised To Challenge The $360 Resistance
data.description:
UNH stock: substantial margin recovery potential

I last covered UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH, UNH:CA) on Feb 5, 2026. That article rated the stock as Buy after reviewing its FQ4 2025 earnings report (ER). Since then, there have been a few notable developments around the healthcare sector as a whole and also specific to the UNH stock. The remainder of this article will detail my thoughts on 3 of them: the company’s upcoming FQ1 2026 ER on April 21st, the changes in Medicare Advantage rate, and also the stock’s latest technical trading patterns. These developments have led me to see large potential for the stock price to break out the $360 resistance level in the near term and to reiterate my buy rating.

Let me start with the upcoming ER. As seen in the chart below, UNH is scheduled to release its FQ1 2026 ER on April 21. The latest earnings revision reveals a massive profitability inflection point this quarter. To wit, in the previous quarter (with Earnings announced on January 27, 2026), the company continued to suffer a compressed net margin (of around 1.70% only on a normalized basis). The upcoming projections signal a radical recovery. To wit, for the upcoming quarter, the EPS Normalized Estimate is set to surge to $6.62 on total revenue of $109.66B. These projection imply a net margin of 5.53% assuming the same share count as the previous quarter.

Next, I will explain why this recovery is very likely.

UNH stock’s profitability metrics

To better contextualize further discussions, the chart below shows how UnitedHealth’s current profitability compares against peers and its historical norms. I see a clear and significant divergence between both by vertical and horizontal comparisons. As seen, the company’s current margins have been substantially below the sector median (with the exception of its Net Income Margin, NIM) and also its historical averages. Its TTM NIM is only 2.69%, which, only about half of its own five-year average of 5.19%.

Against this background, you can see why the FQ1 implied NIM caught my attention. The implied NIM of 5.53% is not only a massive expansion from its TTM and previous quarter’s margins but also exceeds the long-term average of 5.19%. I expect such an expansion, if confirmed in the upcoming ER, to trigger a sizable revelation of the shares in the upward direction.

UNH stock: tailwinds not fully priced in yet

And I do see plenty of catalysts that could help materialize such an expansion and even further boost its margins in the years to come. The company has been strategically repricing its policies and optimizing its membership lately. In terms of operation, the company has also been working on the use of AI technologies to drive efficiency. I anticipate seeing tangible benefits from efforts in the upcoming ER. Furthermore, the recent rate change announced by the Medicare Advantage program (see the details quoted below) should also provide a nice boost to its EPS in the next few years.

Seeking Alpha news: The Trump administration will increase average Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48%, or more than $13 billion, in 2027, according to Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). The Trump administration in January proposed a payment rate hike of 0.09%, which pummeled shares of insurers that run those plans. The closely watched government payment rate determines how much insurers can charge for monthly premiums and plan benefits they offer and, ultimately, their profits.

Yet, these catalysts are not fully priced in in my assessment. With all the positives just mentioned, UnitedHealth’s current valuation profile presents large odds for further upside in my view. To wit, the stock carries a forward non-GAAP P/E of 17.7 only. This is about 13% below its five-year average of 20.3. By a horizontal comparison, this is on par with the sector median (with a difference of less than 1%) and suggests undervaluation in my given the market leading position of UNH in the sector.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the above fundamental considerations, UNH’s recent technical trading patterns also exhibit several textbook signals for an imminent break out. To start, the stock has successfully overtaken its 200-day moving average (MA) of $308.88 as seen, which itself has ended its long-term downward trajectory and began flattening. These signals suggest to me that traders are becoming increasingly more bullish and are ready to push for another up leg. For this push, I consider the $360 level to be the most likely target given its role of the ceiling during a lengthen consolation period during 2025.

Last but not least, the most bullish sign in my mind is the widening of the Bollinger Bandwidth (BB) as you can tell clearly from the chart. The current share price of $316 is now riding the upper band, signaling powerful upward momentum. At the time of my last writing, the stock prices were largely range bound between a relatively narrow BB. With the recent price rallies, the low and upper BB both began to widen (currently around $257 and $316), opening up more room for further rallies.

There are certainly plenty of downside risks ongoing too. To stay on point, I will just detail two of the downside risks that are most relevant to my above analysis. The first one is the potential of a structural topline stagnation or even a decline. If you recall, my first chart highlights the margin expansion potential judging by the latest earnings revisions. But it is also important for me to point out that these revisions also suggest significant top-line headwind. The revenue projection of $109 for Q1 2026 represents a sequential decline of about 3.5% and no year-over-year growth at all (its topline was also $119 billion for FQ1 2025). Second, the boost from Medicare Advantage rate could be countered by many other factors. A notable factor on my list is the medical utilization rates, especially among senior population. This factor is ultimately unpredictable in my view and has historically fluctuated widely.

To close, this UNH revisit details my thoughts on three new developments since my last writing: its upcoming FQ1 ER, Medicare Advantage’s rate change, and its latest technical trading patterns. There are certainly downside risks. After examining these developments and the stock’s current valuation multiples, I believe the upsides outweigh the downsides. I see large odds for the share price to reach $360 in the near term. Even at $360, the stock’s FWD P/E would only be around 20x, still quite reasonable for a business with UNH’s market positioning.

data.title: Microsoft: Don't Buy The Dip, A 30% Correction Is Still Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

There is a large group of investors who believe that the recent decline in Microsoft (MSFT) stock is a mistake, that the company's market value will not only recover its lost ground but also break new ground. These investors need to ask themselves one question. Are you really willing to pay for MSFT’s current products, even though there are more convenient and cost-effective AI agents that perform the same tasks? What's more, it's cheaper, faster, and requires less effort. This is my first piece on MSFT, but my initial investment thesis was to assess the company's attractiveness for potential purchase amid a possible recovery of its lost ground.

But when I began to put my thoughts into practice through fundamental analysis (specifically, evaluating risks and the company’s potential value based on key multiples), that opinion changed completely. Based on my analysis, which shows potential overvaluation and a possible continuation of the stock’s price correction by up to 30%, it’s not possible for me to recommend buying MSFT. Competition analysis involving AI agents indicates a high potential for a new wave of decline in MSFT’s market value, and therefore my recommendation for this asset is "Sell." I will attempt to examine the company’s prospects in the context of its business model transformation in this article, where the main risks and threats will be highlighted.

MSFT's Strategy and Products

Now, Microsoft is a classic example of a large-scale technological transformation, where they're sacrificing short-term financial performance to build long-term dominance in the AI infrastructure segment. Its main goal is to successfully transition from a "Cloud-First" to an "AI-Agentic" model. Considering MSFT’s technological expertise and knowledge, it’s not just about being a cloud service providers, they want to offer a full-fledged operating system for AI agents. Just yesterday, MSFT confirmed on its official blog that:

At Microsoft, we’re leading the way in exploring how workers can use AI agents to help them save time, automate workflows, and amplify human impact. It’s all part of our journey to becoming an AI-first Frontier Firm.

As part of the strategy, the company is implementing a number of practical measures and solutions designed to move it closer to its primary goal. Firstly, a system of vertical AI integration is being established, whereby Copilot is being deployed at every level, ranging from silicon to application software. Secondly, MSFT is open to major partnerships, chief among them its collaboration with OpenAI, allowing it to increase its PRO to $625 billion, of which 45% comes from this partner’s AI solutions. Finally, its management is constantly working to resolve the pressing "bottleneck" issue, resulting in an order backlog of $80 billion for Azure. Computing infrastructure capacity shortages and limited access to electrical power are the primary reasons for order fulfillment delays. For this reason, MSFT is pursuing an active strategy to scale its own data centers, to which the majority of its CapEx is directed. In 2026, it is projected that capital expenditures will rise to 33% of total revenue, while in 2019 this figure was only 11%.

Analyzing the company's product portfolio, one should divide it into three key segments: Intelligent Cloud, Productivity & Business Processes, and More Personal Computing. Table below provides a summary of which products fall under each segment, what role they play in Microsoft's strategy, how they performed in terms of sales growth based on the latest quarterly report.

Pick the most important Microsoft product, everyone goes with Azure. This is the company's "cash cow," which saw a 58% increase in traffic over the past year. Also, the revenue from cloud infrastructure products grew by 39%, proving that computing power is being used at a high intensity. As part of traditional software products, Copilot has become the default interface, reaching 15 million paid subscriptions—a jump of 160% from the previous year. Given that the company plans to raise prices for Office 365 subscriptions starting in July 2026, revenues should increase, ARPU included (with a total of 450 million paying users).

MSFT's Financial Profile

With MSFT undergoing an active strategic transformation, their financial profile may be more volatile, since both their sales structure and margins are changing. On the whole, it can boast of maintaining high operational efficiency despite an aggressive investment policy. Its key product for sustaining steady revenue growth is the Intelligent Cloud (Azure) segment, enabling it to maintain an EBITDA margin of 48–50%. While Microsoft is increasing CapEx to scale its computing infrastructure, this is being absorbed by the high profitability of its software products. As a result, despite the negative pressure on net profit from rising expenses, its margin remains one of the highest in the industry (33–35%).

One positive aspect of MSFT’s financial profile is its balance sheet, reflecting cash reserves of $89.4 billion. As the company’s credit rating is high (AAA), it can often attract additional financing at lower interest rates. Moreover, Microsoft's current accounts payable are quite low. As the likelihood of further Fed rate cuts diminishes, the company's credit rating becomes increasingly important.

Going back to capital expenditures, we should note that their record growth is linked to the need to finance projects for the building of data centers and power generation facilities. As a result, most of the CapEx is allocated to the purchase of GPUs and power generation components. A significant portion of these expenses can be offset by the company’s own free cash flow (FCF), the latest data showing it at $55.3 billion.

Competition for MSFT from AI

Main reason for the recent decline in MSFT stock is increased competition from AI agents, who offer the market new products designed to perform the same tasks. The company could consequently lose its direct connection with users, adversely affecting revenue growth. As a rule, the advantage of AI agents lies in their ability to automate work processes, in which case Microsoft becomes a seller of computing power. The result is that business margins may decline, leading to a 24% drop in market valuation since November 2025.

On top of that, there's also competitive pressure on MSFT from specialized players offering specific AI solutions. Microsoft is focusing on comprehensive services, whereas its competitors in this category offer higher-quality solutions for specific, more narrow tasks. One advantage of such AI applications is that they are trained on specific data, which allows them to be more deeply integrated into workflows. Copilot is different in this regard, which could cause some customers who want to use effective, focused AI solutions at a lower cost to leave.

The competition from open-source models (Llama 4, Mistral, DeepSeek) is also worth considering. Given their growing popularity, it is possible that MSFT will be forced to engage in lower prices to lower the cost of AI services linked to APIs and computing (while prices for traditional software, conversely, continue to rise). Because open-source models are creating additional competition from "free" AI, the company needs to retain customers, which includes offering more affordable pricing, in my opinion. Because of this, there might be a slowdown in the return on investment when scaling infrastructure.

The Risk of MSFT’s "Infrastructure Trap"

Nevertheless, this is not the end of the risks facing MSFT, since the main threat stems from the "infrastructure trap." Its manifestation is driven by the company’s aggressive business model transformation, becoming more burdensome because of the construction of computing facilities and energy infrastructure. Whereas MSFT was previously highly valued relative to the median multiples of the software segment (thanks to high margins and low CapEx), it is now much more highly valued as an AI infrastructure provider.

As a matter of fact, MSFT is becoming the prototype of a digital midstream company, because its revenue will soon depend to a greater extent on network bandwidth and chips. Record CapEx in 2026 is driven by the need to sustain revenue growth, since the growth of Azure users requires massive financial investments in building data centers, GPU procurement, and power supply. The bottom line is that maintaining the previously high ROIC metrics is becoming more difficult, which is partly due to the activities of competitors. That could lead to a decline in MSFT’s multiples, as they will no longer be in line with tech companies but rather with industrial players.

In essence, the infrastructure trap boils down to the fact that Microsoft has to invest huge sums in chips that become obsolete faster than they can pay for themselves. This means there is a risk that the company will face high capital expenditures on an ongoing basis, resulting in FCF failing to reach shareholders. Any profits generated will need to be continuously reinvested to maintain its current market share. Table below illustrates how each metric changes depending on the company's business model when, rather than Cloud Boom, they become an AI Infrastructure player.

The Risk of MSFT’s "Telecom Scenario"

A more significant concern for MSFT investors, though, would be the possibility that the company, along with many others in the industry, will repeat the fate of telecom companies from the 4G/5G era. These players previously invested hundreds of billions of dollars in network infrastructure, such as towers and frequencies. Yet, it was not the telecom companies that reaped the bulk of the windfall, rather those operating "on top" of the network (e.g., Meta, Netflix, Uber). When applying this story to MSFT, we should understand that if AI agents become independent of a specific cloud, then a product like Azure will turn into a conduit for transmitting computations. Apart from the fact that this could lead to a drop in margins (infrastructure is always cheaper than what runs on top of it), excess capacity of this kind could lead to lower prices.

Considering Microsoft's strategy to position Copilot as a sort of "paid-for-use" service for corporate clients, the subscription-based business model must effectively cover data center depreciation costs. Should this not occur, there will be a reassessment of MSFT, shifting its classification from a "Growth" asset to an "Industrial Value" asset. As a result, key multiple metrics could be adjusted downward. That is why MSFT’s current decline may not be a result of irrational market behavior, rather a manifestation of the market’s realization that the company can no longer grow for free and must be evaluated according to the rules of the real economy.

MSFT Valuation

When evaluating MSFT’s investment appeal, one should consider the forward values of key multiples (EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA) against other competitors: Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Oracle (ORCL), Salesforce (CRM), SAP (SAP), and ServiceNow (NOW).

Medians for EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples are 6.23x and 14.42x, resulting in premiums of 51.20% and 8.53% for MSFT stock. This gives a median premium of 29.86%. These calculations thus indicate a potential decrease in MSFT's market value of up to 30% from its current levels.

Conclusion

My fundamental analysis leads me to conclude that MSFT stock are not attractive at current prices, and therefore my rating on them is "Sell." Please note that this investment view does not mean that I dislike Microsoft’s business or consider it to be terrible. The company is successfully transforming its business model, yet management still faces many critical challenges. In essence, MSFT should adjust its market valuation to reflect different valuation multiples. The rationale is that the company is evolving its role from a technology player to a key participant in the AI infrastructure ecosystem. This is happening as the company’s traditional products face intense competitive heat from highly specialized players and AI companies offering more advanced agent-based applications to meet Microsoft users’ needs.

data.title: Arxis: Critical (Aerospace) Provider Flies Off
data.description:
Shares of Arxis (ARXS) have seen a big boom higher on the first day of trading, as could have been expected for this public offering. Investors seem to like the roll-up story in mission-engineered aerospace components, in which Arxis effectively buys smaller peers at lower multiples, raises prices, derives other efficiencies, and commands premium multiples, just like industry leaders TransDigm (TDG) and Loar (LOAR) have done.

Investors applauded the strategy, yet a 40% move higher on the first day of trading, following a strong pricing process, makes valuations quite demanding here.

This is just like its peers, and while TransDigm historically has been a great stock, I fear the setup here. Frankly, acquisition-driven growth could become more competitive here, with more and more peers employing similar acquisition strategies.

Mission-Critical Engineered Components

Arxis designs and manufactures proprietary, mission-critical electronic and mechanical components that are engineered for cutting-edge performance in extreme environments. Products to think of include products like interconnects, injection modules, door slides, cooled dewars, control bearings, rudder bearings, and many more. Such products are designed to resist extreme vibrations, radiation, and extreme temperatures and are corrosion resistant.

The company generates nearly all of its revenues from so-called proprietary products, serving over 600 aerospace platforms to some 5,000 customers. Nearly half these customers and their associated revenues come from defense & space customers, with the remainder of revenues split between industrial technology and commercial aerospace, in that order.

The company is basically a roll-up story, designed by its sponsor Arcline, which since 2019 has acquired and integrated some 30 companies to provide a single platform that provides more of a one-stop solution to its clients. In fact, some 32 deals have been made, creating a large business now, employing nearly 6,000 workers here.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Arxis aimed to sell 40.5 million shares in a preliminary price range between $25 and $28 per share, with pricing set at the high end of the range at $28 per share. All the shares were sold by the company, which ended up fetching $1.13 billion in gross proceeds.

With some 404 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded an $11.3 billion equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net debt position of around $1.3 billion, based on the pro forma balance sheet, for a $12.6 billion enterprise valuation.

The company posted pro forma revenues of $1.60 billion in 2025, more than doubling compared to 2024, but in between many M&A deals were pursued, so that does not tell us that much. Organic revenue growth appears to have come in at around 9%, a pretty decent number, although it appears largely driven by pricing efforts.

An operating profit of $108 million only tells part of the story, as this comes after a $140 million amortization charge. Adjusted EBITDA metrics came in at $572 million, for margins equal to 35% of sales on that front. Translating this into a realistic earnings number is hard, as it adjusts for no less than $188 million in stock-based compensation expenses and a combined $202 million in depreciation and amortization charges, of which the vast majority are amortization charges.

If I subtract stock-based compensation as a continued expense going forward, adjusted EBIT is seen around $384 million. Applying a 6% cost of debt and applying statutory tax rates would leave net earnings around $230 million in such a calculation, equal to $0.57 per share. Leverage, on the other hand, would be manageable in the low 2s. The $12.6 billion valuation jumped on the first day of trading as shares rose by 39%, or nearly $11 per share, adding about $4.4 billion to the valuation, now trending around $17.0 billion. This values the business at 10 times sales, 30 times EBITDA, and with earnings realistically seen just north of half a dollar per share, that multiple comes in the sixties.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks in the offering are plentiful, including regular risks like valuations, leverage (although not so much), the focus on aviation and thus the impact of higher oil prices on air travel, and maybe some controversy as well.

The latter point focuses on deliberate actions of acquiring business and finding efficiencies, with the latter including pricing actions as well. The 35% EBITDA margins of Arxis are very strong, as the story sounds a great deal like that of TransDigm and, more recently, Loar as well. These peers have been reporting even higher margins, with TransDigm having become subject to political scrutiny about pricing practices as well.

These peers have been employing deliberate roll-up stories, albeit with much more debt and aggressive pricing practices. TransDigm trades around 10 times sales as well but famously has EBITDA margins surpassing half of sales, as Loar trades around similar sales multiples as well, as shares recently have come in a bit.

This makes me hard-pressed to recommend it, with investors aggressively pricing the relative sales multiple to these peers. This comes even as Arxis has not yet closed the margin gap compared to these peers, with estimated earnings multiples in the sixties being rather demanding. All this makes me perfectly comfortable taking a patient approach, starting to observe the developments from the sidelines here.

data.title: EVgo: It Is Getting Very Attractive As Operations Nearing Breakeven
data.description:
Introduction

EVgo, Inc.’s (EVGO) share price performance has been disappointing, even though, from looking at its 2025 performance, the company has been progressing quite well, and at this share price, I am very tempted to start a position, but I will wait it out to see how the next couple of quarters turn out.

Performance

The company’s top-line growth remained quite consistent throughout the year, with a clear upward trend. In the latest FY25 report, EVGO’s revenues were up a whopping 49% y/y. The biggest jump happened in the Ancillary segment in terms of dollars and percentage change, by far. For charging revenues, Retail showed decent performance as well, but not as well as others, although in dollar terms, it performed as one of the best. You can see the breakdown below. Overall, it was a really impressive expansion in the top line in 2025.

In terms of profitability and efficiency, as the company is still relatively new, I am not expecting to see any sort of meaningful profitability, especially in GAAP terms. Here, we can see that the company’s margin profile improved materially in 2025. The biggest improvements came from gross and EBITDA margins. Charging network gross margins were 46%, and adjusted gross margins were 51%. Adjusted EBITDA was positive $25m in Q4. They did quite a bit of financial engineering to get the adjusted EBITDA to be a positive number, but I can’t blame them. You have to attract investors somehow. Even on GAAP terms, EBITDA margin is not too far from being positive.

All these improvements line up with the strong revenue growth and a much better utilization rate across the network. It seems that the company’s business is starting to pick up pace, and its operating model is certainly viable with these early results.

Looking at the company’s financial position, EVGO finished the year with $151m in cash and equivalents, against around $204m in long-term debt. That’s quite a bit of debt, but is it actually worrisome? It is certainly a lot riskier for a company that still is not making a profit, but with such a massive cash pile, I don’t see a lot of risk for now. Also, the interest expense is covered by the interest income from the cash it has, so there is essentially no capital being used to cover the interest expense.

I don’t mind the company taking on this new debt, because it clearly has a good reason for it. EVGO is looking to expand its charging network, which will lead to more revenue and income in the future. The company also seems to be using around $8m in cash flow per year, so with over $151m in cash, the company has enough fuel to last a very long time, and that is if it stops doing anything and just burns cash at the same rate. Of course, that is just in theory because the company is also spending quite a bit of capital on CapEx every year. Just in 2025, it spent close to $117m on CapEx. I’m sure more capital will be raised until it can support its own operations, but someone keeps giving them financing, and it makes sense because the model seems to be very promising.

Overall, I like where the operations are heading. The management has been very proactive in expanding the company’s operations to many more stalls this past year, and it looked like it was a good decision, since operations are becoming a lot more attractive, even if they are not profitable just yet. As the company continues to scale, I am sure we will see the company hitting GAAP profitability.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance for 2026

The management didn’t provide numbers for the upcoming quarter, but they did initiate full-year 2026 guidance, which unfortunately fell slightly short when compared to what the analysts were expecting. The management guided for the top line to be in the range of $410m-$470m, or $440m at the midpoint, which is well below consensus expectations of $478m. So, at the midpoint of the guidance, the top-line growth is around 14.5%, while at the top of the guidance, it is around 22%, which is still quite weak, to be honest.

At least the management acknowledged it in the most recent conference call and framed it as a “transition year” for EVGO and not a structural slowdown. The company emphasized that the second half of the year will be a lot more attractive, as there will be a lot more new stalls online by then. Also, to achieve the growth that is necessary in the long-term, the company is looking to continue to invest in G&A to help with the faster deployment of the stalls. So, this slowdown is more of a timing issue rather than a deceleration, which is a relief to hear. Clearly, that was not quite enough for the investing community as the company’s share price plummeted throughout the year.

More Stalls and Quick

The core growth story remains the same. The company needs to deploy more stalls quickly. This is their main growth lever at the moment. In the latest conference call, the company said they are looking to deploy another 1,400-1,650 stalls across the US, which doesn’t tell us that much on its own. In 2025, the company installed around 1,200 stalls across 47 states, so if we take the number from the lower end of the guidance, we get a 16.6% growth. and 37.5% growth at the top range. This is a decent acceleration overall in my opinion. The company is well on track to double its stall operations. Back when I covered it in 2024, the company expected to build around 7,500 stalls in 5 years, at around 1,500 per year. By then, it already had 3,700 stalls in operation, and now that number sits at 5,100 by the end of 2025.

eXtend also seems to be quite a good revenue segment going forward. It has shown decent growth this year, as we saw, 34.5% y/y. Building out such infrastructure for someone else is not their core operations, but 30% of total revenues is nothing that we can ignore. This segment seems to be doing just as well as its core operations, and it will depend on whether the company can continually win more partner contracts to expand the reach of EVs across the country. So, the big question is, will there be enough demand for such an infrastructure upgrade? That all depends on the government to push EVs as the transportation of the future. If there are no incentives, there is no real rush.

Federal programs like NEVI and the Charging and Fueling Infrastructure grant program have already put billions toward EV charging buildout. The political agenda of the current administration in the U.S. may have slowed things down for now; however, it looks like the transition is inevitable.

Closing Comments

I am getting a lot more interested in the company at these prices, especially when looking at the improving fundamentals. I am quite interested in starting a position; however, I will wait for a couple more quarters before my mind is completely made up. The company is doing great at building out the infrastructure, and I truly believe the transition will come eventually, but as I said, the next few years might be a little slow.

The company still has a lot of growth in the U.S., but I wouldn’t be surprised if the management eventually announces global expansion. Europe is a lot more open to EVs.

data.title: Sell Before May And Go Away: Why Another Big Market Drawdown Is Likely In 2026
data.description:
On March 30, 2026, I wrote a bullish article suggesting that the stock market plunge over the Iran war was overdone and that a sharp rebound was likely. I felt the market had become too oversold and I saw the extreme fear levels from investors as being a buying opportunity. That was lucky timing, because the market has rocketed higher since that day and the S&P 500 Index (SPY) and other market indexes have surged by about 11% since that article was published just over two weeks ago. A gain of about 11% is a strong return for most investors for an entire year, so it is really impressive to have this type of gain in just a couple of weeks.

I think the market is way ahead of itself now even if the Iran war issue fades. I see other events on the horizon that the market could start to focus on in the months ahead which could create more volatility and more pullbacks. I think this is a good time to re-evaluate risk levels and sell some stocks in order to cash in on recent gains. Here are some of the points of concern that I have now that the market has rocketed higher:

I Have Concerns About Seasonal Factors

There is an old adage that is based on one of the most well-known seasonal patterns with the stock market, and it says "sell in May and go away". It is based on the idea that the stock market often underperforms during May to October timeframe, while the best six months for the stock market are often from November to April. There are a few reasons why this seasonal pattern tends to occur and it includes the belief in this adage which causes many investors to sell, which potentially creates a self-fulfilling prophecy. But it could also be due to the fact that many professional traders take off for vacations in late Spring and during Summer months and this results in lower trading volumes.

Since 1945, history has shown that November through April are the best 6 month period for stocks with an average gain of about 7%, with the May through October period providing average gains of just over 2%. With a war in Iran, oil prices surging and midterm elections coming up, I think it makes sense to sell stocks even before May, which is why I chose the title for this article.

Latest Job Report And Interest Rate Policy Could Be Increasingly Unfavorable For Stocks

Just recently, the jobs report for March 2026 was released and it was better than expected. Nonfarm payrolls rose by 178,000 which was far better than estimates of just 59,000. The unemployment rate declined slightly to 4.3%. This seems to be good news in some ways because at first glance, it reverses some concerns about the recent weakening trends in the job market and worries over AI-related job losses. However, many of the job gains were from the health care sector which added 76,000 jobs, but I believe there is more to this story. The health care sector is not at risk of AI-related job losses as much as other sectors, and a big part of the health care job gains were due to striking workers (nearly 35,000) at Kaiser Permanente returning to work in March.

This shows that a big part of these health care job gains were based on a one-time event. In addition, white collar jobs such as in government (18,000 jobs lost) and finance (lost about 15,000 jobs) that are impacted by AI-related job cuts continued to show weakness. Based on this, I believe the trend of AI-related job losses seems to be intact. As shown in the chart below, unemployment as been slowly but steadily rising in the past year or so. The problem I see with this job report is that one month of data is not enough to signal the reversal of a trend, but this job report was also not weak enough to make the Federal Reserve lean more towards cutting rates. Overall, I believe this jobs report is going to make the Fed less likely to lower rates in the coming months, especially as concerns grow over inflation potentially increasing due to the surge in oil prices.

The Chart

As shown in the chart below, the stock market seems to have been topping out for the past few months, but more recently, stocks plunged over concerns about the Iran war and even fell below the 200-day moving average. In the past couple of weeks, stocks have surged and recently hit record highs. The resilience is impressive, but I don't think it would be surprising to see stocks give back some of the recent gains.

Valuations And Margin Debt Are High

Major stock market tops tend to occur when investors are complacent, margin debt is high and valuations are elevated. All of this appears to be true today, especially with stocks recently hitting record highs. When these factors are present, stocks could be at risk of a major decline which resets valuations back down to historically normal levels. At this time, margin debt remains near record high high levels, with March data showing that investors have borrowed over $1.2 trillion to buy stocks.

Oil Shocks Often Lead To Recessions

Since World War 2, almost every oil shock has led to a recession in the United States. The potential issue with the current conflict in Iran is duration, because what seemed like a 2 or 3 week conflict is dragging out, which is creating more potential damage to the economy. Oil is such a huge part of the economy that nearly every part of it is impacted, and this is why higher oil prices can lead to inflation and diminish consumer demand as prices rise. In response to higher inflation, the Federal Reserve often raises interest rates which diminishes demand even further, especially for high-ticket items like cars and housing.

I think it is worth noting that in July 2008, the price of Brent oil surged to around $147 per barrel. High oil and commodity prices are thought to be one of the triggers for the recession that turned into the Great Financial Crisis or "GFC". Brent oil is now trading for about the same price that it hit just before the stock market began plunging in September 2008. This is one of the most recent examples of how high oil prices which were often caused by events in the Middle East, have preceded or resulted in major economic downturns, but here are some others:

1973: The OPEC Embargo led to high oil prices and caused a recession between 1973 to 1975.

1979: A revolution in Iran led to high oil prices and a recession hit the economy between 1980 and 1981.

1990: The Gulf War led to high oil prices and there was a recession between 1990 and 1991.

We saw high asset prices which included a housing bubble and rising oil prices, just before the Great Financial Crisis. Today, the housing bubble is even bigger, and oil prices recently hit record levels not seen since just before the GFC. With all of this in mind, a prolonged spike in oil prices could be what triggers another recession on Main Street USA, just as it has in the past.

Even If The Conflict Ends, Aftershocks From The Iran War Could Soon Be Felt

To me, it looks like the conflict seems to be dragging out, even if it has de-escalated with the recent ceasefire. I worry that the longer this drags out, the bigger the economic aftershocks will be in a variety of ways. Of course, oil prices have surged, but fertilizer and helium prices have also soared. High fertilizer prices will likely impact farmers and crop prices going forward. A recent CNBC article suggests that food will soon cost more at your local grocery store. Helium is a key component in the chip manufacturing business and that could create challenges for that industry. Jet fuel shortages are said to be getting critical in some parts of the world. Consumers are already paying much more for gas now and this could get worse in the coming weeks. Airlines have already announced fuel surcharges, but now even companies like Amazon have also just announced a fuel surcharge of 3.5%.

Rising oil prices and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are clearly starting to impact many areas of the global economy. Another potential aftershock of the war in Iran could be rising interest rates. Mortgage rates have jumped since the start of the war and the important Spring home selling season appears to be in jeopardy. Higher gas prices and interest rates could lead to consumers cutting back on buying high-ticket items such as cars or other discretionary items. General Motors (GM) recently announced a sharp decline in sales, and Restoration Hardware (RH) also recently gave weak guidance due to rising interest rates and other economic concerns.

Earnings season is underway, and even if earnings are solid, we might get weak guidance from some companies as higher oil prices and other aftershocks begin to impact the economy. I think we could see major issues arise for many parts of the global economy if Europe runs out of jet fuel which is something the IAE recently suggested as a possibility in about 6 weeks.

Here Are A Few More Concerns I Have When It Comes To The Stock Market

1) There is a history of the stock market "testing" new Federal Reserve chairmen. This happened in October 2018 when Jerome Powell made hawkish comments about interest rates and the stock market responded by plunging about 20%. Alan Greenspan was tested when he took this position as well. As I wrote in this article, bond yields surged shortly after he became Fed chair due to concerns that he would not be tough on inflation, and he was tested again during the 1987 stock market crash.

With President Trump suggesting Jerome Powell could be fired, and with the potential for the market to test Kevin Warsh after he presumably takes the helm at the Fed in May, this could lead to major volatility. This is another reason why I am thinking sell before May and go away could be an ideal strategy. While some investors and analysts have started to speculate about whether the stock or bond market will test Kevin Warsh, I don't think most investors have this on their radar yet, and I like to act before potential market events occur.

2) Midterm election years tend to see large pullbacks. This concerns me that we are not only getting a new Fed chair this year who could be tested but it is also a midterm election year. On average, midterm election years see peak to trough corrections of about 17.5%. We just had a pullback of about 10%, but this did not seem to be deep enough to take this concern off of my list of worries.

3) The fear has turned to greed. When I called for the stock market to rebound a couple of weeks ago, the CNN Fear & Greed Index was at extreme fear levels. When this happens, I try to force myself to buy stocks. But, now this index is in the "greed" level, and that is another reason why I am taking some profits by selling stocks.

Where I Might Be Wrong

Even though I expected a sharp rebound in stocks a couple of weeks ago, I am surprised at the speed and strength of this rally. With stocks now at new highs, it could be a sign that this market has more upside momentum than I give it credit for. This means I could be leaving money on the table by potentially selling too early. I have concerns about guidance, but I do expect earnings results to be generally positive and this could also be good for stocks and help the recent upside momentum continue. If Iran becomes a more stable country when it comes to oil supplies, and decides to take the route that Venezuela has seemingly taken, production could rise and oil prices could drop significantly. If oil declines due to a "peace dividend," this could boost the global economy and the stock market while also giving the Fed less to worry about with inflation. Lower oil prices could lead to lower interest rates which would be bullish for stocks.

In Summary

Even though I expected a big market rally when stocks were at the lows a couple of weeks ago, I am concerned that investors seem to be celebrating the "end" of the Iran war possibly too early. Even if a peace deal works out, I expect aftershocks from the Iran war to play out later this year. I also think all the focus on the Iran war has taken the focus on some other potentially very major market events which could create significant volatility. I see this as including a new Fed chair being tested, and the typical drawdown that is seen during midterm election years. I think investors could latch onto these other market concerns fairly soon, even if the Iran war worries fade. I am very pleased with the recent gains, but I have been taking profits once again because I see market challenges directly ahead of us. I want to be positioned to buy stocks the next time investors are willing to give me bargains and when investors are fearful.

data.title: Ubisoft: An Opportunity Lies Hidden Under Years Of Underperformance
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Growing Value as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction to Ubisoft

Founded in 1986 in France, Ubisoft Entertainment SA (UBSFY) (UBSFF) is a publicly traded video game developer and publisher with international footprint, with studio presence in countries such as France, Canada, United States and China to name but a few. It is widely known for its video game franchises, amongst the most famous of which belong titles like Assassin’s Creed, Far Cry, Rainbow Six, Rayman and Prince of Persia.

Trading currently at a depressed valuation, I believe it presents an intriguing buying opportunity for a medium to long-term oriented risk tolerant investor. While currently being unprofitable due to suboptimal managerial decisions made during the past 5 years, changes in leadership, promised reduction in spending and ownership of valuable assets in the form of well-known video game franchises leading to an attractive pipeline of upcoming releases could all result in powerful catalysts.

Causes of the Fall in Share Price

Recently, the company made headlines with its share price dropping over 30% in January. This was the market's reaction to the announced cancellation of 6 of its projects and a delay in the release of another 7, pushing a meaningful rise in earnings further into the future. Simultaneously an expected loss of roughly €1 billion for the fiscal year ending in March 2026 has been communicated by the management, a shocking result, considering that breakeven was expected prior to this announcement.

Unpleasant as it may have been, the latest underperformance was only the result of the deep flaws that hindered Ubisoft since at least 2021. The unprecedented financial success of Assassins Creed: Valhalla, partially fueled by lockdown during the pandemic, together with cheap debt available in the low interest rate environment, resulted in suboptimal utilisation of excess capital by Ubisoft's management. The studio needlessly expanded and many low quality projects were greenlit around this time (Champions Tactics: Grimoria Chronicles, Star Wars: Outlaws etc.), leading to missed expectation targets, diminished ROIC, and damaged reputation.

From 2023 onwards, Ubisoft started implementing cost cutting measures including layoffs. Bad project execution continued, however, with titles such as Skull and Bones, Avatar: Frontiers of Pandora and XDefiant all missing internal expectations. It is important to note that none of these were traditional or core Ubisoft IPs, I will further elaborate on this observation later.

Most severe changes were announced in January 2026, these were the aforementioned delays and cancellations, closures of several studios, internal reorganisation into “Creative Houses” (individual studios with certain autonomy), and new quality control measures, further details of which have not been disclosed.

Now that we have a better idea of the general situation, I will proceed with listing the positive aspects of the company.

The Value in Ubisoft

It can be stated as a fact that the latest earnings were disappointing. The higher than expected quarterly net bookings of €338 million underline the ability of the company's core franchises to generate revenue. They were, however, entirely overshadowed by a massive expected €1 billion full-year operational loss, caused partially by a €650 million write-off of projects in development that have been cancelled. This allows the company to focus on higher quality and more profitable projects, something which is key to this thesis and is elaborated upon below.

The most attractive attribute of Ubisoft is undoubtedly its valuation. With a market cap of around $590 million as of writing this article. In comparison Electronic Arts Inc. (EA), a company with a similar number of employees (roughly 15000 to Ubisoft's 17000), commands a market cap of over $50 billion. CD Projekt SA (OTGLY), with only around 1000 employees, is valued at over $6.5 billion. The economies of various companies in the gaming industry are very diverse, so comparing market shares is not very relevant on its own. Nonetheless it provides us with one useful piece of information — while many others are priced for excellence, Ubisoft is priced for failure. Market has already managed to price in the announced loss and downsizing, and with the current price being around 3 times the 2023 earnings, it is clear that the company is not expected to return to similar levels of profitability anytime soon.

The discrepancy in the valuation could most likely be ascribed to fears caused by cash burn, and further discount applied due to bad mentioned mismanagement, with the shock caused by restructuring being the most recent and pronounced factors. Competition with a long track record of stable returns and profits is able to command the valuation Ubisoft is not able to, not currently at least. The market seems to have misplaced potential catalysts in the form of strong potential of Ubisoft's IPs, plans to reduce costs and changes in internal organization and management.

To help us assess the value of Ubisoft's assets, we can look at the deal with Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY) from November 2025. Tencent, even while already owning 9.99% of the company directly, was willing to pay Ubisoft €1.16 billion for a 26.3% stake in Vantage Studios, the most performant of the 5 newly created Creative Houses. In paying what was at the time the market cap of the whole studio for just a portion of one of the creative houses indicates Tencent sees the incredible potential this studio has, potential which stems from Ubisoft's most valuable asset: its core IP. (Please note that Tencent also could not obtain shares in the company as a whole, as it is contractually obliged not to do so until 2030).

Aforementioned Vantage Studios works on veritable flagships. Titles such as Assassin's Creed (AC), Far Cry, and Rainbow Six are not only widely known, but more importantly, have historically been able to generate outsized returns. To elaborate in detail on each one is beyond the scope of this article, though I will use Assassins Creed as an example to better illustrate the ongoing changes in Ubisoft, which should be of interest to potential shareholders. The leadership in place during the last 5 years has been replaced, the current creative director being Jean Guesdon, a Ubisoft veteran who has been responsible for both the commercially successful classic Assassins Creed: Black Flag (34 millions unique players from its release in 2013 to 2025) and highly innovative Assassins Creed Origins (40 million players from its release in 2017 to 2025), proving he is capable of successfully departing from the classic formula without impairing the goodwill and earning potential that the IP carries. Among currently planned projects under his supervision are the remake of AC: Black Flag and a new entry in the AC franchise. If we were to assess Guesdon's ability to execute based on his past results, we would invariably have come to the conclusion that these projects will result in a commercial success.

During the last five years, the reputation of Far Cry and Rainbow Six has not been affected in any meaningful manner, with the latest Far Cry entry surpassing sales expectations and the player count of substantially monetized Rainbow Six being stable. Other of the Creative Houses have their own IPs with much smaller, though not inconsequential earnings potential, Anno and Rayman are just a few examples. Profitability of these studios depends much more on the success of Ubisoft cost cutting efforts, results of which we've yet to see.

Another valuable asset that Ubisoft owns are the perpetual cloud streaming rights to all existing videogames made by Activision Blizzard, including those yet to be made up until 2038 in all countries outside EU. The one undisclosed one time fee is already paid in full, though further variable fees will be present in the future. Activision Blizzard makes products with mass appeal for the general audience, which is also the main target audience for cloud streaming, with Asia being the fastest growing market, the EU exception should not be much of a hindrance. The cloud streaming market is expanding at 40% CAGR and should reach over 63 billion USD in 2030 by current estimates. The deal was demanded by the regulators during the acquisition of Activision Blizzard by Microsoft, otherwise such rights would not be sold at all. It is difficult to imagine how this could develop to Ubisoft's detriment.

With free cash flow being -400mil EUR, 670mil EUR in cash and 1.130mil EUR total in current assets on the balance sheet, the company has sufficient liquidity for 2 to 3 years of operations at the current rate of cash burn. It is important to note that should liquidity issues occur, a deal with Tencent would most likely be made. Historically, Tencent has already helped to prevent a hostile takeover from Vivendi in March of 2018, invested in Guillemot Brothers Limited (GBL - holding firm of the founder brothers) in 2022 and acquired a stake in Vantage Studios in November 2025.

Having established that with the valuable assets owned, restructuring underway and Tencent backing, the upside is both large and entirely possible, let us move to the potential hurdles and risks.

Risk Factors

The most severe issue plaguing Ubisoft is mismanagement. Other problems Ubisoft has faced would have been entirely preventable were better management policies in place. This is already well recognised by the market, being the chief reason the company trades at the value of a distressed asset. Nevertheless this could continually be a strong headwind should the reorganisation fail to tackle it fully. No matter how valuable the asset or large the cashpile, bad execution will make outsized earnings elusive. Same applies to cost cutting measures, the company must manage to reduce expenses without harming the bottom line, values of both of which should be watched closely to evaluate the success of these procedures.

Liquidity risk is in this instance made greater by the execution risk, if the company fails to restructure and cut costs efficiently, the investor would have to rely on a hypothetical deal with Tencent.

Another risk for potential investors applies to the whole industry. Earnings and cash flows are incredibly hard to predict in gaming companies, as they rely on the sales of future products to fickle customers, with reputation among consumers often playing as large of a part as the quality of product offered. Accuracy of valuation we might attempt is also invariably affected.

Further contraction in valuation is therefore entirely possible. Even if, based on the transaction in the November 2025 Tencent deal, we value Vantage studios at €3.8 million and therefore come to the conclusion that the value of the rest of Ubisoft is negative, market can still push this negative value even lower, should prospects of more effective operations not materialize.

The restructuring itself could take longer, create additional costs, or make the delay of announced projects last longer.

Tencent itself may prove a risk to the investor, from 2030 onwards, it could potentially attempt a takeover and make the company private, limiting potential upside. Further Tencent investments in Vantage studios would also be unwelcome, as that means dilution of investors ownership in the most valuable of Ubisoft's assets.

Projections and Estimates

So far I have focused primarily on the qualitative factors, but any investment thesis should be supported by at least a few calculations and projections. As was already mentioned, the gaming sector is difficult to predict on a quantitative basis, so the best we can do is estimate various scenarios and approximate their likelihood.

In the most optimal scenario, cost cutting measures are a resounding success, increasing margin without meaningfully harming potential earnings. Reorganization into creative houses leads to better utilization of capital and in turn, better returns. Ubisoft manages to adequately leverage its IPs and restore the trust of its consumers. This occurs in the next three years, before the cash burn causes irreversible damage.

The promised €200 million reduction of cost leads us to the operating income of around €330 million. Let's also assume the new Assassin's Creed and Far Cry projects and cloud gaming increase revenue to 2.4 billion (around the levels it reached in the fiscal year ending March 2024, the year Skull and Bones and the smaller, less ambitious AC: Mirage was released). We are thus presented with operating income of roughly €460 million. If we also assume that all other expenses stay constant and the dilution of shareholders occurs at similar rate, the resulting net income of around €245 million and EPS of €1.7, meaning Ubisoft now trades at P/E of 2.3x of its 2028/2029 (depending on the release schedule, which was not made known) earnings. Return to profitability should also be marked with valuation expansion. When applying the 1.9x average Price/Sales ratio of the last 5 years, we can project the increase in market cap to €4.56 billion and share price of $37.5 when the current rate of dilution is applied. I consider this scenario unlikely, yet entirely possible.

The likeliest scenario in my opinion is that of a moderate success. The cost cutting measures reduce the spending by the intended amount, yet the revenues revenues will not climb sharply as suboptimal execution persists and higher quality standards improve returns only marginally. The market will reflect upon this by lower expansion of valuation multiples. If we assume the next projects to only maintain the effect AC:Shadows had on revenue and thus project the current TTM revenue figure of €2.21 billion to the next three years. We arrive at operating income of €330 million, €114 million net income and EPS of 2.32. When applying a more conservative Price/Sales of 1x, the 2029 share price should be around $18.3 per the ADR UBSFF (noting though that the more liquid ticker is UBSFY).

The most negative scenario consists of persisting bad execution in combination with utter failure of cost cutting, both leading to reduction in sales. Higher than expected cash burn also created liquidity issues, potentially forcing Ubisoft to sell more of Vantage, its most valuable Creative House, to Tencent. The only silver lining for the investors in this situation is the eventuality of Tencent takeover attempt beginning in year 2030 (the year in which Tencent's obligation not to acquire more shares expires), which could in turn lead to better utilization of mentioned valuable assets in conjunction with tender offer at the price favorable to investor. As this case presents by far the most unknowns, conducting any calculation would not lead to meaningful results, but only make pure speculation seem more credible. I consider this as likely as the most optimal scenario, meaning not highly probable, but entirely in the range of possibilities.

In the meantime, that is, until one of these scenarios gets to play out, the market should react positively if there appear signs the company is stabilizing. A return to a more rational valuation should occur if Ubisoft can simply meet its own lowered guidance. As the execution risk discount subsides, investors should see a slight recovery. Return to pre January share price is unlikely, as then the expectations were not lowered by the new guidance. It is therefore reasonable to conclude with a 12 moths price target around 6 USD.

Final Summary

To summarize, Ubisoft is a company that faced and is still facing major issues, most of them stemming from less than ideal management of its consumers. These issues have been recognized and are worked on, the company owns enough cash to be able to succeed with its restructuring, even though results are uncertain. Ubisoft possesses many valuable IP and a streaming license, which are assets with strong potential to create outsized returns. Delays and cancellations have more than likely been already priced in by the market. Tencent is an ambivalent factor that could both provide financial safety and be a cause of dilution. Future Tencent involvement may lower downside in the case of a hypothetical tender offer. Complete Tencent takeover and its taking the company private would however cap the potential upside. Exact earnings projections are impossible, lowering our ability to value the company appropriately. I rate the company as a speculative buy, especially for risk tolerant and long-term oriented investor, willing to suffer through current negative earnings for the future potential of outsized returns facilitated by high value assets.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
